duration: 120sec. the 1st time (not fully in view the whole time); <2sec, the 2nd time; heard only, 3rd tim
specific_location: Greenlee Preserve / Waneka Lake. In willows along north shore of Waneka Lake. At extreme eastern edge of the dense vegetation along north shore of Waneka Lake
description: Here is the unedited description that I posted to CoBirds on the date of observation:  I first saw the Prairie Warbler in the mist/drizzle earlier today. It was foraging low in the willows along the north shore of Waneka Lake, and I was right away struck by its small size, active demeanor, bright yellow underparts, and olive upperparts. My immediate guess was Blue-winged Warbler, but then I put my binoculars on the bird, and saw its muted, but distinctive facial pattern: olive-green cap, broad yellow supercilium, thin brown trans-ocular line, and broad yellow subocular patch bordered below with brown.  The bird had a long tail with large white tail spots, and it twitched its tail frequently. Its wing-bars were indistinct. Head on, the bird had an unmarked yellow breast with fine black streaks along the sides. The bird's flight call was a thin, pure-tone seet. After the bird had disappeared from view, I heard what I believe was its call note: a repeated, musical chep. I briefly saw the bird about 1 hour after the original observation, and I believe I heard its call note again shortly thereafter. I think it was a male, although a rather muted one--maybe a one-year-old with some retained formative plumage?  Before consulting Andrews & Righter, by the way, I would have guessed that spring records of Prairie Warbler would have been earlier than today's bird, but that's not the case: 3 of the 5 records are later than May 20th, including both Boulder County records given by A&R (May 30-31 May 1975 and 5-6 June 1982).Behaviors: Note: Behaviors are described above, under "Description." Key behaviors noted:  1. Tail twitching 2. Foraged in low, scrubby vegetation. 3. Foraged actively.Call: Song not heard. Flight call heard (see below). Call note probably heard too (see below).Plumage: first-cycle male with retained formative
